passages.text:
Not like
A lone beautiful bird
These poems now rise in great white flocks
Against my mind's vast hills
Startled by God
Breaking a branch
When His foot
Touches
Earth
Near
Me.
passages.context: Use when someone expects the divine to arrive grandly. Hafiz's God arrives like a foot breaking a branch — startling, ordinary, close. The poem itself is startled into being. Use to reframe where the sacred shows up: not in monuments but in small disturbances of the ordinary.

passages.text:
When
The violin
Can forgive the past
It starts singing.
When the violin can stop worrying
About the future
You will become
Such a drunk laughing nuisance
That God
Will then lean down
And start combing you into
His
Hair.
When the violin can forgive
Every wound caused by
Others
The heart starts
Singing.
passages.context: Use when someone is locked in the past (grievance) or future (anxiety). The violin cannot play while it holds what it is not playing. The singing begins with forgiveness of wounds and release of tomorrow. The reward is absurd intimacy — God combing you into his hair — Hafiz's way of saying: the released heart becomes indistinguishable from the divine.

passages.text:
We have not come here to take prisoners,
But to surrender ever more deeply
To freedom and joy.
We have not come into this exquisite world
To hold ourselves hostage from love.
Run my dear,
From anything
That may not strengthen
Your precious budding wings.
Run like hell my dear,
From anyone likely
To put a sharp knife
Into the sacred, tender vision
Of your beautiful heart.
We have not come here to take prisoners
Or to confine our wondrous spirits,
But to experience ever and ever more deeply
Our divine courage, freedom, and
Light!
passages.context: Use when someone has made themselves hostage — to an obligation, a fear, a relationship that diminishes them. Hafiz's direction is startlingly specific: run. Not reflect, not process — run from what knifes the heart. The purpose of existence is not endurance but the deepening of freedom and joy.

passages.text:
I
Have
Learned
So much from God
That I can no longer
Call
Myself
A Christian, a Hindu, a Muslim,
A Buddhist, a Jew.
The Truth has shared so much of Itself
With me
That I can no longer call myself
A man, a woman, an angel,
Or even pure
Soul.
Love has
Befriended Hafiz so completely
It has turned to ash
And freed
Me
Of every concept and image
My mind has ever known.
passages.context: Use when someone is defending a label — religious, philosophical, psychological — as though it were the thing itself. Hafiz's dissolution goes beyond religion to gender, to 'soul' itself. The endpoint: love has burned every concept to ash. Use to invite beyond whatever container the person is protecting.

passages.text:
Even
After
All this time
The sun never says to the earth,
'You owe
Me.'
Look
What happens
With a love like that,
It lights the
Whole
Sky.
passages.context: Use when love has become transactional — when giving comes with an implicit ledger. Hafiz offers the sun as the image of love without accounting. The consequence of accountless love is not its exhaustion but its expansion: it lights the whole sky. The move: what would love look like if it kept no record?

passages.text:
It used to be
That when I would wake in the morning
I could with confidence say,
'What am 'I' going to
Do?'
That was before the seed
Cracked open.
Now Hafiz is certain:
There are two of us housed
In this body,
Doing the shopping together in the market and
Tickling each other
While fixing the evening's food.
Now when I awake
All the internal instruments play the same music:
'God, what love-mischief can 'We' do
For the world
Today?'
passages.context: Use to describe what happens after awakening — not a grand dissolution but a domestic intimacy. The divine becomes a roommate, a fellow shopper, a tickling companion. The 'I' doing things alone gives way to a 'we' doing love-mischief. Use to introduce the possibility that union isn't transcendence but closer partnership.

passages.text:
Fear is the cheapest room in the house.
I would like to see you living
In better conditions,
For your mother and my mother
Were friends.
I know the Innkeeper
In this part of the universe.
Get some rest tonight,
Come to my verse again tomorrow.
We'll go speak to the Friend together.
Your soul and my soul
Once sat together in the Beloved's womb
Playing footsie.
Your heart and my heart
Are very, very old
Friends.
passages.context: Use when someone is living inside fear as though it were the only available room. Hafiz offers a real estate metaphor: fear is the cheapest, meanest accommodation — and you can move. He also asserts pre-existing friendship as the ground of the invitation: your soul and mine have been companions since before birth.

passages.text:
Like
A pair
Of mismatched newlyweds,
One of whom still feels very insecure,
I keep turning to God
Saying,
'Kiss
Me.'
passages.context: Use when the spiritual path has become too dignified, too composed. Hafiz keeps this urgent, embarrassing, honest. The insecure newlywed asking to be kissed is the real spiritual posture — not authority or arrival but need. Use to give permission for undignified spiritual longing.

passages.text:
God
Disguised
As a myriad things and
Playing a game
Of tag
Has kissed you and said,
'You're it—
I mean, you're Really IT!'
Now
It does not matter
What you believe or feel
For something wonderful,
Major-league Wonderful
Is someday going
To
Happen.
passages.context: Use when someone is trapped in belief anxiety — 'do I believe enough? do I feel the right things?' Hafiz dissolves the question: God has already tagged you. It doesn't matter what you believe or feel. Something major-league wonderful is coming regardless. The move: shift from qualifying for grace to being already marked by it.

passages.text:
I rain
Because your meadows call
For God.
I weave light into words so that
When your mind holds them
Your eyes will relinquish their sadness,
Turn bright, a little brighter, giving to us
The way a candle does
To the dark.
I speak
Because every cell in your body
Is reaching out
For God.
passages.context: Use when the question is: why speak, why make, why write? Hafiz's answer: because your body is already crying out for something — and the poem is rain meeting that cry. Language here is not representation but nourishment, not description but the thing itself arriving.

passages.text:
Once someone asked me,
'Why do saints seek divine annihilation
And are often humble
And like to spend their free time
Upon their knees?'
I replied,
'It is a simple matter of etiquette.
When ones goes into a mosque or temple
Is it not common to remove what
Covers your
Feet?
So too does it happen
With this whole mind and body—
That is something like a shoe sole—
When one begins to realize
Upon Whom you are really standing,
One begins
To remove the shoe from the
Temple.'
passages.context: Use when annihilation or self-dissolution seems exotic or dramatic. Hafiz reduces it to etiquette: you remove your shoes at the door of the sacred. The mind and body are shoes. When you realize what you are standing on, the removal happens naturally — not as violence but as courtesy.

passages.text:
If God
Invited you to a party
And said,
'Everyone
In the ballroom tonight
Will be my special
Guest,'
How would you then treat them
When you
Arrived?
Indeed, indeed!
And Hafiz knows
There is no one in this world
Who
Is not upon
His Jeweled Dance
Floor.
passages.context: Use as an ethical thought experiment with immediate application. If you knew everyone in the room had been personally invited by God, how would your behavior change? Hafiz's point: they have been. The dance floor is always jeweled. Use to shift from abstract ethics to concrete social presence.

passages.text:
You carry
All the ingredients
To turn your life into a nightmare—
Don't mix them!
You have all the genius
To build a swing in your backyard
For God.
That sounds
Like a hell of a lot more fun.
Let's start laughing, drawing blueprints,
Gathering our talented friends.
passages.context: Use when someone is using their capacities to construct suffering — not out of fate but out of habitual mixing of the wrong ingredients. Hafiz acknowledges you have the genius for misery — and redirects it. The swing for God: use your creativity for joy instead of suffering. Same materials, different project.

passages.text:
The
Earth
Lifts its glass to the sun
And light—light
Is poured.
A bird
Comes and sits on a crystal rim
And from my forest cave I
Hear singing,
So I run to the edge of existence
And join my soul in love.
I lift my heart to God
And grace is poured.
An emerald bird rises from inside me
And now sits
Upon the Beloved's
Glass.
I have left that dark cave forever.
My body has blended with His.
passages.context: Use when union needs an image rather than a definition. Hafiz gives it in cascade: earth lifts to sun, light pours; I lift heart to God, grace pours. The emerald bird rises from inside — the innermost self becomes the thing perched on the divine. The cave is left forever. Use to name what spiritual opening feels like from the inside.

passages.text:
Art is the conversation between lovers.
Art offers an opening for the heart.
True art makes the divine silence in the soul
Break into applause.
Art is, at last, the knowledge of
Where we are standing—
Where we are standing
In this Wonderland
When we rip off all our clothes
And this blind man's patch, veil,
That got tied across our brow.
passages.context: Use when someone asks what art is for, or whether their creative work matters. Hafiz: art is lovers talking. It makes the silence applaud. It is the knowledge of where you are when the veils come off. Use to give weight and purpose to creative acts that feel frivolous or insufficient.

passages.text:
I cannot sit still with my countrymen in chains.
I cannot act mute
Hearing the world's loneliness
Crying near the Beloved's heart.
My love for God is such
That I could dance with Him tonight without you,
But I would rather have you there.
Is your caravan lost?
It is,
If you no longer weep from gratitude or happiness,
Or weep
From being cut deep with the awareness
Of the extraordinary beauty
That emanates from the most simple act
And common object.
passages.context: Use to diagnose spiritual numbness. The sign that the caravan is lost: you can no longer weep from gratitude or from beauty. Hafiz locates the sacred not in grand religious experience but in being cut by the beauty of ordinary things. Use when someone reports feeling nothing — ask when they last wept at something simple.

passages.text:
When
No one is looking
I swallow deserts and clouds
And chew on mountains knowing
They are sweet
Bones!
When no one is looking and I want
To kiss
God
I just lift my own hand
To
My
Mouth.
passages.context: Use as the most compact statement of divine immanence in the Hafiz corpus. When you want to kiss God, you lift your own hand. The body is already the sacred body. Use when someone is looking for God at a distance — to redirect the gaze inward, to the immediate, to the already-present.

passages.text:
There is a private chamber in the soul
That knows a great secret
Of which no tongue can speak.
Your existence my dear, O love my dear,
Has been sealed and marked
'Too sacred,' 'too sacred,' by the Beloved—
To ever end!
Indeed God
Has written a thousand promises
All over your heart
That say,
Life, life, life,
Is far too sacred to
Ever end.
passages.context: Use when someone is facing death — their own or another's — or when existence feels fragile and unjustified. Hafiz's answer is not philosophical argument but a sealed mark: your existence has already been declared too sacred to end. The thousand promises are already written. Use as comfort that doesn't explain but simply insists.

passages.text:
Don't
Surrender
Your loneliness so quickly.
Let it cut more
Deep.
Let it ferment and season you
As few human
Or even divine ingredients can.
Something missing in my heart tonight
Has made my eyes so soft,
My voice so
Tender,
My need of God
Absolutely
Clear.
passages.context: Use against the reflex to escape loneliness immediately — through distraction, connection, consolation. Hafiz says: let it do its work. Loneliness seasons in ways nothing else can. The proof: it makes the eyes soft, the voice tender, the need of God absolutely clear. Use when someone is fleeing their own emptiness.

passages.text:
Love is
The funeral pyre
Where I have laid my living body.
All the false notions of myself
That once caused fear, pain,
Have turned to ash
As I neared God.
What has risen
From the tangled web of thought and sinew
Now shines with jubilation
Through the eyes of angels
And screams from the guts of
Infinite existence
Itself.
passages.context: Use when love is being treated as comfort or possession rather than transformation. Hafiz names it: love is a funeral pyre. You lay your living self down. The false notions burn. What rises shines through angels. The move is not to frighten but to honor what love actually does — it destroys what isn't real and lets what is real rise screaming from the guts of existence.

passages.text:
We
Are not
In pursuit of formalities
Or fake religious
Laws,
For through the stairway of existence
We have come to God's
Door.
We are
People who need to love, because
Love is the soul's life,
Love is simply creation's greatest joy.
passages.context: Use when religion or philosophy has become a set of rules to satisfy rather than a living movement toward love. Hafiz: we are not here for formalities. We have climbed the stairway of existence and come to a door. The only qualification is that we need to love. Use to cut through spiritual bureaucracy.

passages.text:
The earth braces itself for the feet
Of a lover of God about to
Dance.
The sky becomes very timid
When a great saint starts waving his arms
In joy,
For the sky knows its prized fixtures,
The sun, moon and planets
Could all wind up
Rolling so wild on the floor!
My dear, this world, its laws,
Our perceptions,
Are such a minute part of existence.
Should not all of our suffering and sadness
Be like this:
As just dropped from an infant's palm
That is asleep against the breast
Of God?
passages.context: Use to shift scale when suffering feels total. The universe braces for the dancer — the sky goes timid when joy erupts. Suffering, in this scale, is like something dropped from an infant's hand while sleeping against God. Not minimized but proportioned. Use to give suffering its actual size relative to the scale of existence.

passages.text:
How
Do I
Listen to others?
As if everyone were my Master
Speaking to me
His
Cherished
Last
Words.
passages.context: The Hafiz prescription for listening: treat every person as your teacher delivering their final teaching. This is a cognitive practice, not a sentiment. Use when conversation is happening at the surface — when listening is really waiting to speak. The move: what if these were the last words you would hear from them?

passages.text:
How
Did the rose
Ever open its heart
And give to this world
All its
Beauty?
It felt the encouragement of light
Against its
Being,
Otherwise,
We all remain
Too
Frightened.
passages.context: Use when someone is closed — to intimacy, to expression, to risk. The rose opens because light encouraged it. Nothing else works. The question becomes: what is the encouragement this person needs to feel against their being? And: are you providing it? Use to redirect from demand to encouragement.

passages.text:
What
Do sad people have in
Common?
It seems
They have all built a shrine
To the past
And often go there
And do a strange wail and
Worship.
What is the beginning of
Happiness?
It is to stop being
So religious
Like
That.
passages.context: Use when grief has solidified into a cult. The shrine to the past is not grief — it is something stranger: a ritualized return to what was lost, a worship that keeps the wound fresh. The beginning of happiness is not achievement or healing but simply stopping the pilgrimage. Use to name when mourning has become maintenance.
